Q1: I tried to organize my study routine around my hands-on learning style, so I'd block out chunks of time where I could really dig into practical exercises and work through problems physically. With 12 online courses, I had to be strategic about spreading my 21 hours across the week. I'd usually dedicate mornings to watching lecture videos and then spend afternoons actually doing things—writing out notes by hand, working through practice problems, sometimes even pacing while I reviewed material out loud.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what felt most urgent at the time. I tried to keep some consistency, but honestly with limited resources and so many courses to juggle, I'd often have to shift my focus to whatever deadline was breathing down my neck. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to make tough choices about what to prioritize.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check—new assignments posted, discussion deadlines, or feedback on work I'd submitted. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what my peers were discussing because those interactions really helped me feel less alone in the struggle. The fear of missing something important was honestly a big motivator for me to check in regularly.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, logging in became almost automatic for me, like checking my phone in the morning. The peer support aspect really helped cement that habit because I genuinely looked forward to seeing what others were saying and contributing my own thoughts. Having those 49 peer interactions made the LMS feel less like a chore and more like a community space where I could connect with people going through the same challenges.
Q5: The discussion forums and video lectures were honestly lifesavers for me. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so I'd watch the videos and then immediately try to apply what I learned, pausing frequently to work through things myself. The peer discussions were incredibly valuable because hearing how others approached problems gave me new angles to try, and explaining my own thinking helped solidify my understanding.
Q6: I'll be honest, I sometimes skipped over the supplementary reading materials, especially PDFs that were just walls of text. With my learning style, I need to be doing something, not just passively reading. I'd also delay some of the optional quizzes if I was already overwhelmed with required work—my assignment completion rate wasn't great because I had to make hard choices about where to put my energy given my limited resources.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what I needed to know for assignments or exams, or when it involved actually doing something rather than just absorbing information. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because that social element made learning feel more meaningful. If something felt too abstract or disconnected from practical application, I'd struggle to justify spending time on it.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which was nice, but also less urgency to actually complete them. I'd sometimes start optional activities with good intentions but then abandon them when required work demanded my attention. The required ones had this weight to them that made me prioritize them even when I was scared or overwhelmed, while optional ones were easier to let slide when things got tough.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and watch them on my commute or when my internet was acting up. I kept handwritten notes that I'd review constantly, and I'd work through problems on paper because that physical act of writing helps me learn. I also borrowed textbooks from the library since I couldn't always afford to buy my own materials.
Q10: There were so many times I was deeply engaged with the material but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd spend hours with my handwritten notes, walking around my apartment reciting concepts, or working through practice problems on scrap paper. Sometimes I'd form informal study groups with classmates where we'd meet in person and work through difficult concepts together—none of that showed up in any system.
Q11: Oh definitely, I did this all the time. I'd download a lecture video once and then watch it three or four more times offline, pausing and rewinding to really understand the tricky parts. My internet isn't always reliable and I couldn't afford to waste data re-streaming things, so downloading and studying offline became my normal approach. The LMS would only show that single access, not the hours I spent with that material afterward.
Q12: There were definitely moments when fear really got to me and made it harder to engage. When I was scared about falling behind or not understanding something, I'd sometimes freeze up instead of pushing through. Around midterms, I felt particularly overwhelmed trying to balance everything with limited resources, and that's when my attendance and assignment completion suffered most.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me, especially juggling 12 courses with limited financial resources—I couldn't always afford to just focus on studying. When I was exhausted or stressed about money, I'd still log into the LMS but my engagement quality dropped significantly. I might watch a video but not really absorb it, or scroll through discussions without meaningfully participating.
Q14: Yes, this happened more than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open and be technically watching a lecture, but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about bills, thinking about other deadlines, or just mentally exhausted. I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes without actually learning anything. It felt like going through the motions without being truly present.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially when I was scared of not doing them well enough. If an assignment felt particularly challenging, I'd sometimes avoid starting it because the fear of failure was paralyzing. I knew it was important, but the anxiety made it easier to tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was in a better headspace. This is probably why my assignment completion rate was lower than my actual assignment scores.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd become much more active in the days leading up to due dates, sometimes cramming in video watching and discussion posts at the last minute. The structure helped in some ways because it forced me to engage, but it also meant my activity was very spiky rather than consistent. I wish I could have been more steady, but the pressure of deadlines was what actually got me moving.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on me. When I got positive feedback on my assignments—and I scored pretty well when I actually submitted them—it boosted my confidence and made me more eager to engage with the next module. But if feedback was delayed or critical, I'd sometimes retreat and feel scared to continue. The peer support intervention really helped because getting encouragement from classmates kept me going even when formal feedback was slow.
Q18: The course structure with its regular deadlines and discussion components really shaped how I participated. Having those 11 discussion posts as part of the course pushed me to engage with peers, which ended up being one of the most valuable parts of my learning experience. But the structure also felt overwhelming at times with so much content across my 12 courses—I had to be selective about where I invested my energy.
Q19: The video content was great for my learning style because I could pause, rewind, and actively work through things at my own pace. The peer interaction features made engagement easier because I felt supported and less alone. What made it harder was when there was too much reading-heavy content or when the workload assumed I had more time and resources than I actually did. More hands-on activities would have been ideal for how I learn best.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to learn and engaging with all the materials, but then life circumstances prevent them from actually completing and submitting the work. Maybe they're dealing with financial stress, family obligations, or just running out of time because they're juggling too much. The engagement is real, but the output doesn't match because something breaks down between learning and producing.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam score and assignment scores were decent even though my completion rate wasn't perfect. I think it's because I was doing a lot of deep learning offline that didn't show up in the system. Someone could watch a video once, really understand it, and then study from their notes for hours—the LMS would show minimal activity but the learning was substantial.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, how often you engage with different resources, and whether you're meeting deadlines. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and all the offline learning that happens. They can't capture whether I actually understood something or just clicked through it. They also miss the emotional experience—the fear, the breakthroughs, the support from peers that happened outside the formal system.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely wrestling with the material, not just passively consuming it. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, applying concepts, and connecting ideas to things I already know. For me, it also includes the social dimension—feeling connected to my peers and like we're learning together. True engagement is when the course matters to me personally and I'm invested in actually understanding, not just completing tasks.
Q24: The LMS data would never show how scared I felt sometimes, or how the peer support intervention genuinely helped me push through difficult moments. It wouldn't capture my handwritten notes, my offline study sessions, or the informal conversations with classmates that clarified confusing concepts. It also wouldn't show the financial stress that affected my participation, or how much effort it took for me to engage meaningfully despite having limited resources. My engagement was much deeper than any log could reflect.
